compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
[G4 container review — parent 4fcd, child 22bf | sibling cross-check: a36b, 5a6c] One finding (mode: scope overlap). The other 6 cross-child modes read clean (details below).

FINDING — Scope overlap (this child 22bf ⟷ sibling a36b `worm-folly-barge`)
- criteria: parent AC1 ("a mechanism detects material drift in the code the review was grounded against — e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files"); parent AC3 ("tested: code drift after a signed review → attestation no longer valid").
- location: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` `claim_gate_check` step-3 (whole-HEAD exact-match staleness check, lines 95–102); the same behavior is the subject of bug a36b.
- evidence:
  - 22bf description names the fix explicitly: "Content-addressing makes this … immune to … shared-checkout HEAD moves — the specific `worm-folly-barge` complaint" and AC2: "Claim SUCCEEDS when an unrelated file changed after signing (fixes `worm-folly-barge`)".
  - a36b's ACs prescribe the SAME remedy: AC1 "Claim freshness is scoped to the code the review was grounded against (resolved edit-set / cited files) rather than the whole HEAD sha"; AC2 "A relevant change … STILL invalidates"; test AC "review at X, commit an unrelated file, claim succeeds; review at X, commit a cited file, claim is blocked."
  - Both target the identical edit (`claim_gate_check` step-3 replacement) and carry near-verbatim, overlapping test scenarios (unrelated-file → succeeds; dependency/cited-file → blocked). a36b is currently an independently-open bug ticket (status: open), not a closed report folded into 22bf.
  - This same overlap was independently flagged by an earlier G4 pass in a36b's comment thread; recording it here on the implementing child so the duplication is visible from both sides.
- scenarios: (a) Both 22bf and a36b get independently claimed/implemented → two competing edits to `claim_gate_check` step-3 and duplicate/conflicting test suites for the same unrelated-file-succeeds / cited-file-blocked behavior. (b) a36b is implemented with a narrower whole-HEAD-tweak that diverges from 22bf's content-addressed `{path: content-hash}` design → inconsistent fixes for one root cause.
- impact: Risk of duplicate or conflicting implementation of one behavior across two open sibling tickets; ambiguous ownership of the `worm-folly-barge` fix and its tests.
- checklist_item: scope overlap.
- suggested_fix: Make exactly one ticket own the scoped-freshness implementation + tests. Either (i) convert a36b into a non-implementing bug that 22bf "fixes" (fold its repro + unrelated-file/cited-file scenarios into 22bf's AC6 test set as verification), or (ii) add an explicit `fixes`/blocking link a36b→22bf and strip a36b's prescriptive implementation ACs, leaving it as the reproduction/acceptance witness. Either way the `claim_gate_check` step-3 change and its test scenarios live in one place.

The other 6 modes (high-confidence, benign-reading filter applied):
- Implicit shared state — CLEAN-ish: 22bf and 5a6c both extend the SIGNED manifest in `attest.py` `build_manifest`/`sign_plan_review` (22bf: `{path: content-hash}` map; 5a6c: per-finding code-hash vintage + reuse map). This is a coordination point, but 5a6c `depends_on` 22bf and is sequenced atop 22bf's manifest format, so it reads as additive layering, not a contradiction. Not flagged.
- Conflicting assumptions — CLEAN: 5a6c explicitly affirms 22bf's dependency basis ("The dependency basis stays Story 1's proxy set (`file_impact` ∪ citations)") and rejects observed-read-set capture; a36b prescribes the same scoping. No contradiction.
- Dependency gap — CLEAN: 5a6c declares `depends_on` 22bf; a36b declares `relates_to` 22bf. No missing edge.
- Ordering violation — CLEAN: 22bf is Story 1 (always FULL re-review on drift; narrowed delta explicitly out of scope → Story 2); 5a6c is Story 2 consuming 22bf's drift detection. Correct ordering.
- Consumer impact — CLEAN: the sole consumer (`src/rebar/_commands/claim.py:109`) reads only `check.get("ok")` and the generic `reason`; replacing the `stale-head` verdict internals does not break it. `head_sha`/`signed_at` retained as audit metadata. No dangling consumer.
- Residual references — CLEAN: child retains `head_sha` as audit metadata and removes only the exact-match gate; no orphaned reference introduced by this child.

Verified against code: `attest.py` material_fingerprint already binds the DECLARED `file_impact` list (orchestrator.py:678–689) but NOT on-disk file CONTENT — consistent with 22bf's premise that today's binding is whole-HEAD/declared-metadata, not grounded-code content. P4 (`det_floor.py:309–326`) checks only the file-impact UPPER bound (`fi > P4_FILE_IMPACT_SOFT_CAP`), not empty — confirming 22bf's "P4 does not already cover empty" claim, so the new P9 advisory does not overlap P4.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body: Plan-review triage (PASS, 0 blocking): remediated 6→3 advisory across two passes (content-hash algo/source, sign-time citation extraction, P9→registry.py, signed-map-authoritative claim check, perf-measurement test, per-path manifest shape). Remaining 3 advisory judged adequately addressed, not churned further: (1) f7d81 ADR-in-Scope — ADR docs/adr/0002 already authored, listing it in Scope is cosmetic; (2) f49757 perf bound — AC already mandates a measuring test with a single-digit-ms bound, exact fixture is an impl detail; (3) f8e002 concurrent working-tree mutation between sign/claim — design fails SAFE (claim hashes current working tree; any in-flight difference conservatively invalidates). Claiming on the current valid attestation rather than invalidating it for cosmetic edits.

compiled_state.description:
## Why

The plan-review attestation's freshness is currently bound to the exact code HEAD sha
(`attest.py` `claim_gate_check` step 3: `result.head_sha != head` → `stale-head`). This is
too coarse in BOTH directions:

- **Over-invalidation (child bug `worm-folly-barge`):** ANY commit — even an unrelated file or a
  docs typo — invalidates a freshly-earned, still-correct attestation, forcing a slow (~3-5 min),
  billable re-review for no semantic reason. Especially painful on multi-ticket branches and
  shared-checkout test runs that move HEAD.
- **Under-invalidation (this epic's core goal):** the binding is to the WHOLE commit, not to the
  code the review was actually grounded against, so the design also fails to model "the cited code
  drifted" precisely.

Fix: scope attestation freshness to **what the review depended on, not who/what changed the rest of
the tree** — the content of the dependency files — using a content-addressed (Merkle) binding. This
matches the consensus of every sound incremental-staleness system (Gerrit `copyCondition`
`has:unchanged-files`, Bazel input-hashing, proof-caching dependency checksums). No per-commit
attribution, so the "sibling exemption" concern dissolves: a sibling's edit to a shared file is
treated exactly like any other edit.

## What

- **Dependency set** = union of the ticket's declared `file_impact` and the files cited in the
  review's findings (whole-file granularity). The cited files are extracted **at sign time from the
  in-memory verdict** — each finding's `citations[]` entries with `kind == "file"` (the
  `CITATION_KINDS` vocabulary in `findings.py`) contribute their `path`. They are NOT read back from
  the persisted `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar, whose `_slim` form drops citation paths; and they are never
  needed at claim time (the claim check reads the signed map, below).
- **Content-addressed binding:** at sign time, compute a `{path: content-hash}` map of the
  dependency set and store it **inside the signed manifest** (`build_manifest` / `sign_plan_review`)
  so drift detection is tamper-evident. (Today `head_sha` is recorded as audit metadata but is NOT
  in the signed payload — this also closes that latent integrity gap.)
  - **Hash algorithm/format:** SHA-256 over the **raw file bytes** (no newline/whitespace
    normalization); binary and large files are hashed the same way (no special-casing). A path in the
    set that does not exist on disk is recorded with a fixed `"absent"` sentinel hash, so a later
    deletion (absent→present or present→absent) is a detectable change.
  - **Content source:** the **working-tree, on-disk** file content (what `review-plan` actually reads
    when it grounds the review), at BOTH sign time and claim time — NOT the committed git-blob at
    HEAD. This is why the binding is HEAD-move/rebase immune: only the bytes the review saw matter.
- **Drift check at claim:** replace the whole-HEAD exact-match (`claim_gate_check` step 3) with a
  per-file content-hash comparison. Any mismatch / deletion / unexpected-creation of a dependency
  file = drift → block the claim (invalidate), prompting re-review. Content-addressing makes this
  ancestry-independent (immune to rebases and shared-checkout HEAD moves — the specific
  `worm-folly-barge` complaint).
- **Empty-set conservative fallback:** when the dependency set is empty (no `file_impact` AND no
  citations — common; this very epic has `file_impact: []`), we cannot scope, so fall back to
  invalidate-on-any-commit (today's safe behavior, but now ONLY for unscopable tickets).
- **New named DET-floor check (`P9` file-impact coverage):** `det_floor.py` currently has P1–P8; P4
  checks only the file-impact UPPER bound (`fi > cap`), not empty. Add a new, explicitly named check
  that, for **leaf tasks only** (`applies_at`), emits an **advisory finding (never blocks)** when
  `file_impact` is empty, advising the caller to specify the `{path, reason}` files this work will
  touch — so the attestation can be scoped to them (otherwise any commit invalidates it) and
  `next_batch` can schedule it conflict-free. Register it in `CANONICAL_DET`, the completeness guard,
  and the module docstring.

## Scope

- `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` — `build_manifest` / `sign_plan_review` (embed the signed
  `{path: content-hash}` map); `claim_gate_check` (replace step-3 whole-HEAD match with the per-file
  drift check; `head_sha` / `signed_at` remain audit metadata only).
- `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/det_floor.py` — new `p9_file_impact_coverage` advisory check, added to
  the `DET_CHECKS` tuple + the module docstring.
- `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py` — add `"P9"` to the `CANONICAL_DET` tuple and the
  completeness guard (these live in `registry.py`, NOT `det_floor.py`).
- `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py` — resolve the dependency set at sign time
  (`file_impact` via `get_file_impact` ∪ the verdict findings' `kind=="file"` citation `path`s).
- Tests under the plan-review test suite.

Out of scope (Story 2): the narrowed delta-re-review on drift. This story always falls back to a
FULL re-review when drift invalidates.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The attestation binds a SIGNED `{path: content-hash}` map of the dependency set
      (`file_impact` ∪ review-cited files); the whole-HEAD exact-match staleness check is removed.
- [ ] Claim SUCCEEDS when an unrelated file changed after signing (fixes `worm-folly-barge`); claim
      is BLOCKED when a dependency file's content changed or was deleted (closes under-invalidation).
- [ ] Empty dependency set → conservative invalidate-on-any-commit fallback.
- [ ] New explicitly named DET check (`P9`) emits an advisory (non-blocking) warning on empty
      `file_impact` at leaf tasks, with guidance text; registered in `CANONICAL_DET` + completeness
      guard + module docstring. (Confirmed P4 does not already cover empty.)
- [ ] The drift check is content-addressed (rebase / HEAD-move immune) and cheap; a **test measures**
      it (times `claim_gate_check`'s drift step over a representative dependency set, e.g. ~30 files,
      and asserts it stays in low single-digit ms) and asserts the claim path makes no LLM/network
      call — the perf claim is verified, not just asserted.
- [ ] The signed manifest serializes the dependency binding as a **per-path `{path: content-hash}`
      map** (NOT a single rolled-up root hash), so Story 2 (`grace-thud-feint`) can attach a
      per-finding code-hash vintage + reuse map on top of it. This payload shape is the contract
      fixed in ADR `0002-code-drift-invalidation.md`.
- [ ] **Claim-path check reads the SIGNED map, not a re-derived set.** At claim time the drift check
      iterates the `{path: hash}` entries IN the signed manifest and re-hashes exactly those paths —
      it does NOT recompute `file_impact ∪ citations` from current ticket state. (Re-deriving would
      let a post-sign `file_impact` shrink silently drop paths from the check and evade drift
      detection; the signed map is the authoritative, tamper-evident set.)
- [ ] **Citation extraction is schema-correct.** The "cited files" half of the dependency set is
      taken from the structured citation `path` (`kind == "file"`) on the **in-memory verdict's**
      findings at sign time (the persisted `REVIEW_RESULT` `_slim` sidecar drops paths, so it is NOT
      the source); a citation with no resolvable `path` is ignored (fall back to `file_impact`)
      rather than guessed. Verify the actual findings/citation schema and
      use the structured field; if none exists, that gap is part of this story's work.
- [ ] Tested: review at X, commit an unrelated file → claim succeeds; commit a dependency file →
      claim blocked; delete a dependency file → blocked; empty-set ticket → any commit blocks; `P9`
      fires on empty `file_impact` and is silent when populated.

file_impact.reason: sign dependency-file content-hash map into the manifest; replace whole-HEAD staleness with per-file drift check
new P9 empty-file-impact advisory check + register in CANONICAL_DET/completeness-guard/docstring
resolve dependency set (file_impact union citations); wire P9 applies_at leaf

compiled_state.title: Code-drift detection + invalidation: content-addressed attestation freshness scoped to the review's dependency files (+ P9 empty-file-impact DET warning)
